--- title: "Task: Introduction to R" number-sections: false --- [← Back to session page](../02_intro-r.qmd) :::{.callout-note} Find the solution here after the session ends. ```{=html}

Click here for the solution.

``` ::: ## Getting started Copy and paste the following three vectors into your R script: - `species`: name of the species - `bodywt_kg`: body weight in kg - `brainwt_kg`: brain weight in kg ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species <- c( "MountainBeaver", "Cow", "GreyWolf", "Goat", "GuineaPig", "Diplodocus", "AsianElephant", "Donkey", "Horse", "PotarMonkey", "Cat", "Giraffe", "Gorilla", "Human", "AfricanElephant", "Triceratops", "RhesusMonkey", "Kangaroo", "GoldenHamster", "Mouse", "Rabbit", "Sheep", "Jaguar", "Chimpanzee", "Rat", "Brachiosaurus", "Mole", "Pig" ) bodywt_kg <- c( 1.4, 465, 36.3, 27.7, 1.0, 11700, 2547, 187.1, 521, 10, 3.3, 529, 207, 62, 6654, 9400, 6.8, 35, 0.1, 0.02, 2.5, 55.5, 100, 52.2, 0.3, 87000, 0.1, 192 ) brainwt_kg <- c( 0.0081, 0.423, 0.1195, 0.115, 0.0055, 0.05, 4.603, 0.419, 0.655, 0.115, 0.0256, 0.68, 0.406, 1.32, 5.712, 0.07, 0.179, 0.056, 0.001, 0.0004, 0.0121, 0.175, NA, 0.44, 0.0019, 0.1545, NA, 0.18 ) ``` ::: ## Variables and vectors 1. What is the 13th species in the vector? 2. Get the species at positions 6, 13, and 14. 3. How many species are in the dataset? Save the number in a variable called `n_species`. - Hint: try the function `length()` ## Vector arithmetic 4. Calculate the brain-to-body weight ratio for all species and save it in a new variable called `ratio`. 5. Convert the body weight from kg to grams and save it in a variable called `bodywt_g`. ## Functions and missing values 6. Calculate the mean body weight and the mean brain weight. What happens for brain weight and how do you fix it? - Hint: check `?mean` 7. Also try `sum()` and `median()` on brain weight. ## Optional tasks (if you finish early) You can do these in any order — or skip them and just take a break. - Use the `round()` function to round the `ratio` vector to 2 decimal places. - Use `?round` to find out which argument controls the number of decimal places. - Try `min()`, `max()`, `sum()`, and `sd()` on `brainwt_kg`. Do they all need the same fix for missing values? - Use `length()` and `sum()` together with `is.na()` to find out how many missing values are in `brainwt_kg`. - Hint: `is.na()` returns `TRUE` for each `NA` value. What happens if you `sum()` a logical vector?
